coredata.dc:title: Advanced dual-channel sensing of coumarin with phosphorescent carbon dots: Mechanism insights
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiankang
Lin, Liuquan
Zhou, Sen
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) show the highlighted advantages in chemical sensing and bioimaging owing to its long lifetime, however, acquiring the efficient phosphorescence of carbon dots is still encountering difficulty due to the spin prohibition. Here, we synthesized one type of CDs through one-step hydrothermal method. To be specific, the CDs displayed the blue fluorescence in aqueous, and emitted the bright green phosphorescence when being coated on the filter paper. Importantly, introducing coumarin to the CDs resulted in the distinct quenching of both its fluorescence and phosphorescence. Based on this phenomenon, we successfully established a dual-channel detection of coumarin. The fluorescence detection channel exhibited a linear range of 5–350 μM with a detection limit of 0.196 μM, while the phosphorescence detection channel achieved a linear range of 50–1000 μM with a detection limit of 0.268 μM. The further investigation revealed that IFE and SQE were mainly responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CDs, while the phosphorescence quenching was originated from the increased energy gap between the singlet and triplet states (ΔEST) of CDs, thus obstructing the ISC process. Moreover, we employed the CDs as the phosphorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Coumarin
Dual-channel detection
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs coated on the filter paper
Painting with the phosphorescent ink of CDs
Investigating the selectivity and anti-interference of detecting coumarin by CDs
Luminescent response of CDs to coumarin
Selectivity and potential interference of CDs detecting coumarin
Establishing the dual-channel assay of coumarin by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of synthesizing CDs; (B) Timelapse of the phosphorescence for CDs; (C) Mechanism of CDs detecting coumarin through the dual-channel.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, inset: CDs with daylight (a) and UV on (b); (B) Fluorescent 3D spectrum of CDs; (C) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs, inset: CDs powder with daylight (a), UV on (b) and UV off (c); (D) Phosphorescent 3D spectrum of CDs; (E) CIE coordinates of fluorescence and phosphorescence for CDs; (F) Fluorescence quantum yield of CDs; (G) Fluorescence and (H) phosphorescence lifetime decay curves of CDs.

(A) HR-TEM image of CDs; (B) Size distribution of CDs; (C) UV absorption spectrum of CDs; (D) FTIR spectrum of CDs; (E) XPS survey of CDs; high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (F), N 1 s (G), O 1 s (H), S 2p (I) for CDs.
(A) Images of CDs powder with UV on or off; (B) Images of painting Chinese characters on filter paper with UV on or off.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs+coumarin (inset: images of CDs without (a) or with (b) coumarin under UV); (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of coumarin; (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of CDs and concentrations of coumarin; (D) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs, CDs+coumarin (inset: images of CDs without (a) or with (b) coumarin once UV was switched off); (E) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of coumarin; (F) Linear relationship between phosphorescence intensity of CDs and concentrations of coumarin.

(A) UV–vis spectra of CDs, coumarin or CDs + coumarin; (B) FTIR spectra of CDs without or with coumarin; (C) F0/F fitting curves of CDs with different concentrations of coumarin; (D) UV–vis spectra of coumarin and FL excitation and emission spectra of CDs; (E) Illustration for ΔEST between S1 and T1 of CDs without or with coumarin.

head.ce:title: Advanced dual-channel sensing of coumarin with phosphorescent carbon dots: Mechanism insights

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room-temperature phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) show the highlighted advantages in chemical sensing and bioimaging owing to its long lifetime, however, acquiring the efficient phosphorescence of carbon dots is still encountering difficulty due to the spin prohibition. Here, we synthesized one type of CDs through one-step hydrothermal method. To be specific, the CDs displayed the blue fluorescence in aqueous, and emitted the bright green phosphorescence when being coated on the filter paper. Importantly, introducing coumarin to the CDs resulted in the distinct quenching of both its fluorescence and phosphorescence. Based on this phenomenon, we successfully established a dual-channel detection of coumarin. The fluorescence detection channel exhibited a linear range of 5–350 μM with a detection limit of 0.196 μM, while the phosphorescence detection channel achieved a linear range of 50–1000 μM with a detection limit of 0.268 μM. The further investigation revealed that IFE and SQE were mainly responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CDs, while the phosphorescence quenching was originated from the increased energy gap between the singlet and triplet states (ΔEST) of CDs, thus obstructing the ISC process. Moreover, we employed the CDs as the phosphorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Herein, we synthesized one type of CDs with room-temperature phosphorescence, which used 3-aminopropanesulfonic acid and benzoic anhydride as the precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method. The freeze-dried powder of CDs showed the dual fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions. When coated on the filter paper, it could emit the bright green phosphorescence. Based on its advanced optical property, the CDs was successfully applied as the phosphorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting. Additionally, we found that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence signals of the CDs were quenched once coumarin was introduced, and thus a dual-channel detection of coumarin was established. Our further exploration demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was elucidated as IFE and SQE, while phosphorescence quenching was attributed to ΔEST of CDs increasing. This dual-channel strategy provides a new approach for the facile detection of coumarin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) offers the prominent superiority in biochemical analyses and information anti-counterfeiting benefited from its long lifetime and large Stokes shift [1–4]. Howbeit, obtaining the stable RTP materials generally faces various challenges because of the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions [5–7] and their susceptibility to quenching by oxygen [8–10]. Traditional RTP materials, such as organometallic complexes [11] and metal-free organic compounds [12], typically involve the complicated synthesis processes, high costs and usually exhibit the notable toxicity [13–15]. Therefore, developing new phosphorescent materials that are low toxic and free of metals is considered as a promising issue. Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as the nanoscale carbon materials with excellent optical properties, low toxicity and superior biocompatibility [16–18], which provided the possibility of its applications in information encryption [19,20], biochemical sensors [18,21] and optoelectronic devices [22–24] and other fields [25]. Studies have proved that incorporating the elements like nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) or sulfur (S) during the preparation of CDs could promote the n-π* transitions, enhance spin-orbit coupling (SOC) processes and facilitate intersystem crossing (ISC), thus efficiently achieving the room-temperature phosphorescence emissions [26–29].

Coumarin, an organic compound with a benzopyranone structure, is widely used in perfumes, cosmetics, soaps and detergents on the basis of its unique fragrance. Partial derivatives of coumarin are also utilized as the food additives to enhance aroma and flavor. Nevertheless, excessive intake of coumarin can cause severe headaches, vomiting, dizziness and insomnia, and its higher doses can even lead to central paralysis, respiratory arrest and coma [30,31]. Thus, the detections of coumarin in food and environmental samples is considered to be meaningful. The routine detections of coumarin, mainly including high-performance liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [32,33], usually require the complicated and expensive instrumentation, intricate sample preparation and cumbersome operating procedures, hence limiting their more application scenarios. Consequently, it is necessary to develop the simple detecting methods of coumarin.

Herein, we synthesized one type of CDs with room-temperature phosphorescence through a one-step hydrothermal method, while 3-aminopropanesulfonic acid served as both the N and S source and benzoic anhydride as the carbon source. ( Fig. 1A). The freeze-dried powder of CDs showed the dual fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions (Fig. 1B). When coated on the filter paper, it could emit the bright green phosphorescence because of hydrogen bonds forming between the CDs and the filter paper, thus reducing the non-radiative transitions. Based on its advanced optical property, the CDs was successfully applied as the phosphorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting. Additionally, we found that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence signals of the CDs were quenched once coumarin was introduced, and thus a dual-channel detection of coumarin was established. Meanwhile, the fluorescence detection exhibited a linear range of 5–350 μM with a detection limit of 0.196 μΜ as well as the phosphorescent detection with a linear range of 50–1000 μM. Our further exploration demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was elucidated as IFE and SQE, while phosphorescence quenching was attributed to ΔEST of CDs increasing (Fig. 1C). This dual-channel strategy provides a new approach for the facile detection of coumarin. Compared to the reported studies, the easily obtainable precursors were employed and the reaction conditions were relatively mild in the current work (Table S1).

Typically, Benzoic anhydride, KCl, Coumarin, Dicoumarol, Hymecromone, Methoxsalen, Gelseminic acid, Psoralen, Daphnetin, Umbelliferone and Scopolin were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) as well as L-glutamic acid, D-Mannose, Xylitol, metal ions (Cu2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ba2+, NH4+, Zn+, K+) obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Similarly, Citric acid, Tartaric acid, Methyl alcohol, n-propyl alcohol, N, N-dimethylformamide, acetonitrile, NaOH and Malic acid were bought from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Sodium benzoate was acquired from Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), and Vitamin C was from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of these commercial reagents are analytical grade and were utilized without the further purification.

Basically, we conducted the fluorescence and phosphorescence measurements with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), while UV–vis absorption spectra were captured using a Shimadzu UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). For the elemental and functional group analyses, we utilized an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (New York, USA) and a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained with a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, USA) operating at 200 KV, and dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were recorded by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, England). Likewise, we employed an Edinburgh FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) to identify the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of the CDs. Moreover, the powder of CDs was freeze-dried with a freeze dryer of SCIENTZ-12 N (Ningbo, China), and its synthesis process was finished in a Muffle furnace of ZSX 1200 (Beijing, China).

Firstly, 0.4 g of benzoic anhydride and 0.2 g of 3-aminopropanesulfonic acid were successively dissolved in 3 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was evenly dispersed by the ultrasonic cleaner. Then, the previous solution was transferred to a reaction vessel followed by heating at 230°C for 9 hours, and a solid-liquid mixture was obtained once the reaction completed and being cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged for 5 minutes at 10,000 rpm, and the milky-white solid residue was collected with the supernatant discarded. Next, this solid was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and filtered through 0.22 μm of microporous membrane. Again, the filtered solution was transferred into a 1000 MWCO of dialysis bag and dialyzed for 72 hours. After the dialysis, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the purified carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs coated on the filter paper
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 1 mg of purified CDs powder was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to around 7. Next, 50 μL of the solution was carefully pipetted onto the filter paper of 0.5 cm × 1 cm followed by subjected to an oven of 60°C for 1 hour. After drying, the room-temperature phosphorescence of CDs was recorded using the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Painting with the phosphorescent ink of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mg of CDs powder was first dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and the pH of this solution was also adjusted to around 7. Next, the patterns of traditional solar term in China were drew on the filter paper by gouache brush while the CDs solution served as the phosphorescent ink, and this filter paper was dried at 60°C for 1 hour. After drying, the images recorded with a UV lamp of 365 nm on or off.

As a standard run, the CDs was dissolved with a solution of 75 % ethanol-water, reaching a concentration of 5 mg/mL. In parallel, 100 μL of coumarin solutions with different concentrations (5 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, 80 μM, 100 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM and 350 μM for fluorescence, 50 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 500 μM, 600 μM, 800 μM, 900 μM and 1000 μM for phosphorescence) were separately added into 100 μL of CDs in 1.5 mL of Eppendorf (EP) tubes, and then 800 μL of ultrapure water was added into each tube followed by adjusting the pH to 7. After the incubation at 20°C for 25 minutes, the fluorescence intensity was immediately measured with the excitation of 291 nm. Simultaneously, 50 μL of each solution was dropped onto the filter paper of 0.5 cm × 1 cm, and these papers were heated at 60°C for 1 hour, and the corresponding phosphorescence intensity was measured with the excitation of 301 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the selectivity and anti-interference of detecting coumarin by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of CDs detecting coumarin, 100 μL of the CDs solution was separately added to 1.5 mL of EP tubes followed by adding 600 μL ultrapure water. Then, 200 μL of various compounds (coumarin, dicoumarol, hymecromone, methoxsalen, gelseminic acid, psoralen, daphnetin, umbelliferone and scopolin, each concentration for 0.01 M) were added to the previous tubes, respectively. After the incubation at 25°C for 40 minutes, their fluorescence intensity was measured. At the same time, 50 μL of each solution previously obtained was dropped onto the filter paper followed by thoroughly drying, and the phosphorescence intensity measured.

During the process of evaluating the influence of potential coexisting substances on the detection of coumarin, 200 μL of CDs with 5 mg/mL, 200 μL of coumarin with 0.01 M, 600 μL of ultrapure water and 200 μL of different substances (benzoic acid, glutamate, xylitol, D-mannose, citric acid, vitamin C, tartaric acid, malic acid, Na+ and K+, each concentration for 0.01 M) were separately mixed in 1.5 mL of paralleled EP tubes. Then, the related fluorescence and phosphorescence were measured following the similar experimental procedure for the selectivity tests.

To obtain the carbon dots with the optimal optical property, we optimized the details of synthesis according to its phosphorescence intensity, and 3 g of benzoic anhydride, 230°C and 9 hours were selected as the optimized reaction conditions (Figure S1).

To identify the basic optical property, the maximum fluorescence emission of CDs was initially determined to be 340 nm when excited at 291 nm ( Fig. 2A and Fig. 2B) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 8.82 % (Fig. 2F). The CDs solution was transparent with daylight and exhibited the bright-blue fluorescence under 365 nm of UV lamp. The CDs powder appeared as white with daylight, and showed the bright green RTP with UV off. When fixed on the filter paper, the phosphorescence emission wavelength was 496 nm with the excitation of 301 nm (Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D). Meanwhile, the CIE coordinates of the fluorescence and phosphorescence for CDs were (0.16, 0.01) and (0.13, 0.38), respectively (Fig. 2E). Meanwhile, the emission wavelengths of both fluorescence (Figure S2A) and phosphorescence (Figure S2B) for CDs varied with different excitation wavelengths, indicating its excitation dependence of the CDs. Besides, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was calculated to be 3.73 ns (Fig. 2G) as well as the phosphorescence lifetime of 279.96 μs (Fig. 2H).

To investigate the phosphorescence of CDs, we further analyze its morphology and structure. Firstly, the morphology of the CDs was described by the images of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), which exhibited as the uniform spherical particles in aqueous ( Fig. 3A). The particle size ranged from 2.32 to 3.44 nm measured by dynamic light scattering (Fig. 3B). Also, we recorded its UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 3C), and the CDs exhibited an absorption band at 228 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC. Another absorption peak at 275 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of CO [34]. Next, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were utilized to investigate the functional groups and elemental composition of the CDs. The FTIR spectrum showed the peaks at 3140 cm⁻¹ and 2843 cm⁻¹ (Fig. 3D), representing the stretching vibrations of N-H and C-H, respectively. The peaks of 1670 cm⁻¹ and 1427 cm⁻¹ corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CN and NN, while the peak at 1126 cm⁻¹ was attributed to the stretching vibration of SO. Again, the peak of 1260 cm⁻¹ described the stretching vibration of C-N [35–37].

Meanwhile, the XPS survey revealed that the CDs was primarily composed of C, N, O, and S elements, and their percentages were calculated as 65.00 %, 5.96 %, 23.27 % and 5.77 % [38], respectively (Fig. 3E). To be specific, the C 1 s spectrum showed two peaks at 284.8 eV and 286.2 eV (Fig. 3F), corresponding to C-C/CC and C-O, respectively. And the N 1 s spectrum displayed two peaks at 401.7 eV and 400.0 eV (Fig. 3G), which were originated from C-NC and N-H. In addition, the O 1 s spectrum exhibited two peaks assigned to OC-O (533.2 eV) and C-O (531.6 eV) (Fig. 3H), while the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3I) showed two peaks associated with various types of C-SOX [39,40].

Subsequently, we systematically explored the stability of CDs with various irradiation time of UV, concentrations of sodium chloride, incubation temperature, pH value, organic solvents and metal ions. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of CDs slightly fluctuated with the irradiation time of UV (Figure S3A), concentrations of sodium chloride (Figure S3B), incubation temperature (Figure S3C), pH value (Figure S3D) or organic solvents (Figure S3E) changing. To a certain degree, the metal ions of Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ba2+ showed the effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Figure S3F). As for its phosphorescence, the intensity of CDs gradually decreased with UV irradiation time extending (Figure S4A), while the variations of sodium chloride concentration (Figure S4B), incubation temperature (Figure S4C), pH values (Figure S4D) and organic solvents (Figure S4E) scarcely affected its phosphorescence intensity. Similar to its fluorescence, the phosphorescence intensity was also affected by the metal ions of Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Mn2+ or Ba2+ (Figure S4F). Overall, these results demonstrated the generally satisfactory stability of both fluorescence and phosphorescence for the CDs.

As described in Fig. 4A, the powder of CDs displayed the blue fluorescence with 365 nm of UV and emitted the green phosphorescence for up to 6 seconds with UV off, and we addressed whether the solution of CDs could serve as the phosphorescent ink for painting. Consequently, we drew the Chinese characters representing four traditional solar terms of China on the filter paper (Fig. 4B), and these characters exhibited the stable green room-temperature phosphorescence with while UV was switched off. This data demonstrated the potential of CDs towards anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent response of CDs to coumarin
ce:section.ce:para: Considering its fluorescence and phosphorescence, we addressed that whether the CDs showed the potential of serving as an optical probe, and we found that the addition of coumarin obviously reduced the fluorescent ( Fig. 5A) and phosphorescent (Fig. 5D) signals of CDs. Hence, this phenomenon provided the possibility of establishing a dual-channel detection for coumarin. We accordingly optimized the detection procedures, and the optimal conditions of pH=7 (Figure S5C), 20°C (Figure S5C) and incubation time of 25 minutes (Figure S5A) were selected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and potential interference of CDs detecting coumarin
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of CDs detecting coumarin, we introduced a series of analogues (dicoumarol, hymecromone, methoxsalen, gelseminic acid, psoralen, daphnetin, umbelliferone and scopolin), and the results revealed that these analogues structurally similar to coumarin could also quench the fluorescence (Figure S6A) and phosphorescence (Figure S6C) of CDs. Since the analogues of coumarin are less commonly applied in the fields of cosmetics and food, CDs could still be employed for assaying coumarin. Furthermore, we designed and conducted the experiments to evaluate the potential impact of co-existing substances on detecting coumarin. We separately added the coexisting substances (benzoic acid, glutamate, xylitol, D-mannose, citric acid, vitamin C, tartaric acid, malic acid, Na+, K+) to the CDs in presence of coumarin. The results indicated that these substances hardly affected both the fluorescence (Figure S6B) and phosphorescence (Figure S6D) of the CDs, demonstrating that the dual-channel detection of coumarin by CDs showed the basically acceptable anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the dual-channel assay of coumarin by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the assay of CDs detecting coumarin, we performed the corresponding standard curves. Both the fluorescence (Fig. 5B) and phosphorescence (Fig. 5E) intensity of CDs regularly decreased as the concentration of coumarin consistently increasing. Accordingly, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and concentrations of coumarin ranged from 5 μM to 350 μM (R² = 0.955) with a detection limit of 0.196 μM (Fig. 5C). Importantly, the linear range of 50 μM to 1000 μM (R² = 0.994) with a detection limit of 0.265 μM was achieved during the phosphorescent analysis (Fig. 5F). Thus, we successfully established a dual-channel method to detect coumarin. Compared to the studies reported, the detection method proposed here for coumarin provided the approximate linear range and detection limit (Table S2).

To explore the mechanism of CDs detecting coumarin, we performed the further investigations. Initially, there appeared two new absorption peaks in the UV–visible spectrum of CDs by adding coumarin ( Fig. 6A), and the FTIR spectrum of CDs and coumarin broadened compared that of CDs or coumarin only, both indicating that new covalent bonds such as O-H, N-H formed (Fig. 6B). These results suggested that new compounds formed between CDs and coumarin, thus resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CDs, which is considered as a key characteristic of static quenching effect (SQE) [41,42]. Meanwhile, the F0/F did not show a linear relationship as the concentration of coumarin varied (Fig. 6C), where F0 and F separately represented the fluorescence intensity of the CDs without or with coumarin. This phenomenon did not match the characteristic formula of F0/F−1 = KSV[Q] for dynamic quenching effect (DQE), indicating that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by coumarin was not originated from DQE [41]. Besides, we compared the UV absorption spectrum of coumarin and fluorescence spectra of CDs, and found the obvious overlap between the absorption and the fluorescent excitation (Fig. 6D), proving that the inner filter effect (IFE) contributed to the quenching of fluorescence [43]. Taken together, the fluorescence quenching of CDs was associated with both SQE and IFE.

Furthermore, we also investigated the mechanism of phosphorescence quenching of CDs by coumarin. By acquiring the phosphorescent spectra of CDs without and with adding coumarin, we calculated that the singlet-triplet energy gap (ΔEST) between S1 state and T1 state showed an increase of 0.062 eV after introducing coumarin (Fig. 6E), thus inhibiting the intersystem crossing process (ISC) and further facilitating to the quenching of phosphorescence [44].

In summary, we proposed a hydrothermal method to synthesize the RTP CDs by employing 3-aminopropanesulfonic acid and benzoic anhydride as the precursors. In brief, the CDs emitted the blue fluorescence, and showed the bright green phosphorescence while fixed on the filter paper. On the basis of their optical property, we utilized the CDs as a phosphorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting. Interestingly, it was found that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs were quenched by introducing the coumarin, and thus we successfully established a dual-channel detection of coumarin by virtue of this phenomenon. Additionally, we further investigated the detection mechanism of CDs responding to coumarin, and provided the evidence that IFE and SQE led were responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CDs, and the increased ΔEST of CDs resulted in the quenching of phosphorescence after the introduction of coumarin. Thereby, the dual-channel strategy we built here provided a new approach for the detection of coumarin.

Jiankang Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liuquan Lin: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology. Sen Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaoming Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by SAMR Science and Technology Program (2022MK105, 2023MK102), Chongqing Scientific Institution Incentive Performance Guiding Special Projects (cstc2022jxj120036, CSTB2023JXJL-YFX0027) and Chongqing Municipal Market Supervision Administration Science and Technology Program (CQSJKJDW2023005).

ce:section.ce:para: The Supporting Information is available free of charge at http://pubs.acs.org.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135347.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urea-formaldehyde resin room temperature phosphorescent material with ultra-long afterglow and adjustable phosphorescence performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Xu, B. Wang, S. Liu, W. Fang, Q. Jia, J. Liu, C. Bo, X. Yan, Y. Li, L. Chen, Urea-formaldehyde resin room temperature phosphorescent material with ultra-long afterglow and adjustable phosphorescence performance, Nat. Commun. 15 (2024) 4415.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room-temperature phosphorescent materials derived from natural resources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Luo, B. Tian, Y. Zhai, H. Guo, S. Liu, J. Li, S. Li, T.D. James, Z. Chen, Room-temperature phosphorescent materials derived from natural resources, Nat. Rev. Chem. 7 (2023) 800-812.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lighting up metastasis process before formation of secondary tumor by phosphorescence imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Chang, L. Xiao, Y. Fan, J. Gu, Y. Wang, J. Yang, M. Chen, Y. Zhang, Q. Li, Z. Li, Lighting up metastasis process before formation of secondary tumor by phosphorescence imaging, Sci. Adv. 9 (2023) eadf6757.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic room-temperature phosphorescence materials for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, H. Li, M. Yang, W. Dai, J. Shi, B. Tong, Z. Cai, Z. Wang, Y. Dong, X. Yu, Organic room-temperature phosphorescence materials for bioimaging, Chem. Commun. 59 (2023) 5329-5342.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-color pure organic room temperature phosphorescent materials with long lifetime and high efficiency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Guan, Z. Tang, J. Deng, Y. Zheng, H. Li, X. Liu, Multi-Color Pure Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescent Materials with Long Lifetime and High Efficiency, Adv. Funct. Mater. 34 (2024) 2310198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The current progress and challenges of carbonized polymer dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zheng, S. Tao, B. Yang, The Current Progress and Challenges of Carbonized Polymer Dot-Based Room-Temperature Phosphorescent Materials, CCS Chem. 6 (2023) 604-622.
